segments.s007-g1: تو را uses the personal pronoun تو plus the object marker را (object emphasis), so ‘you’ is the patient of the following verb. دانش و دین is a coordinated noun phrase 'knowledge and religion'. رهاند is a third-person singular present (classical/poetic) meaning 'frees/delivers' and takes تو را as its object. درست is an adverbial qualifier 'properly/ rightly' modifying رهاند.
segments.s007-g2: در رستگاری is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in/within salvation'. ببایدت is an archaic impersonal/obligatory form with the enclitic -ت 'for you' (literally 'it is necessary for you'); it expresses obligation 'you must'. جست is the infinitival/root sense 'seek' (object/complement of ببایدت). Word order is typical poetic: prepositional phrase then the modal verb + infinitive.
segments.s007-g3: وگر is the conditional 'if'. دل is the subject/affected noun 'heart'. نخواهی is the second-person present of خواستن 'to want/wish' used here inside the conditional ('if your heart does not want/wish'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...' and باشد is the subjunctive/present form of بودن 'to be' ('that it be'); نژند is a predicative adjective 'base, dishonorable'.
segments.s007-g4: This clause repeats the verb نخواهی 'you/would not want' followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. دائم 'always, constantly' modifies the following nominal phrase. بوی is literally 'scent/air' used here metaphorically for 'appearance/mark', and مستمند is the noun/adjective 'needy, poor'. The whole phrase functions as the complement of نخواهی: 'you would not wish to be always with the air/appearance of the needy.'
segments.s007-g5: به گفتار پیغمبرت is a prepositional phrase 'by/according to the words of your Prophet' (پیغمبرت = 'your prophet' with enclitic -ت). راه جوی is an imperative-like instruction: راه 'the way/path' followed by جوی, here used as 'seek' (جوی functions as a verbal imperative/verb meaning 'search for'), so literally 'seek the way'. The verb comes after its object due to poetic word order.
segments.s007-g6: دل از تیرگی‌ها is 'the heart from darkness' (دل as the thing to be cleansed). The sequence ب + دین + آب should be read as a prepositional/agentive phrase meaning 'by/with the (religious) water' (ب = with/by, دین = religion, آب = water), i.e. 'with the water of religion' or 'this religious water'. شوی is the second-person present/imperative of شدن 'to become' / 'to be washed' (here 'be washed/cleanse yourself'), so the clause means 'wash/cleanse your heart of darkness with this (religious) water.'
segments.s007-g7: چه is the interrogative 'what'. گفت here carries the idea of speech/utterance ('said' / 'word') and functions to introduce the reported content. آن خداوند تنزیل و وحی is an appositive phrase 'that Lord/master of تنزیل (revelation sent down) and وحی (inspiration)', describing whose words are meant. Word order is inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s007-g8: This repeats خداوند in two parallel appositions: خداوند امر 'the Lord (of) command' and خداوند نهی 'the Lord (of) prohibition'. امر and نهی are nominal complements naming the Lord's authority (to command and to forbid); the repetition is a rhetorical/poetic device.
segments.s007-g9: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/for' introducing the clause. خورشید (literally 'sun') is used as a high-status metaphorical subject; بعد از رسولان is a prepositional phrase 'after the Messengers' (بعد + از = 'after'); مه 'moon' sits next to خورشید as a parallel celestial image (poetic pairing of sun and moon). Word order is poetic: the images (sun, moon) and the temporal phrase (after the Messengers) are foregrounded before the verb in the next segment.
segments.s007-g10: نتابید is the past negative of تابیدن ('to shine'); the initial ن- marks negation, so 'did not shine'. بر کس = 'upon anyone' (بر + کس) and z (ز) is the poetic genitive/prepositional 'from/of' attaching بوبکر; به is a directional/relational particle here. Together the clause means literally 'did not shine upon anyone from/of Abu Bakr (i.e. like Abu Bakr)', with the negated verb closing the poetic clause.
segments.s007-g11: عمر is the subject (the name ʿUmar). کرد is the simple past of 'to do/make', so عمر کرد = 'Umar made/did'. اسلام را uses the object marker را to mark Islam as the definite object; آشکار here is an adjective/adverb meaning 'manifest/clear'. The sequence means 'Umar made Islam manifest.'
segments.s007-g12: بیاراست is a past-form verb 'adorned' (bi- + آراستن), and گیتی is its object 'the world'. چو is the comparative 'like/as', introducing the simile گیتی چو باغ بهار 'the world like a spring garden'. Note the poetic order: verb + object + simile (possibility of ellipsis of an explicit subject because عمر from the previous clause carries over).
segments.s007-g13: پس از is the compound preposition 'after' (پس + از); هر دو means 'both' (i.e. the two predecessors). آن is a demonstrative 'those/that' referring back to the two persons, and بود is the past copula 'was'. عثمان گزین places the name followed by گزین used adjectivally 'the chosen one/selected'; the construction reads 'After both of those, there was Uthmān, the chosen.'
segments.s007-g14: This is a nominative apposition describing Uthmān with two honorific epithets. خداوند here means 'lord/master' (an honorific), شرم 'modesty' (so 'lord of modesty'), و is 'and', then خداوند دین 'lord of the faith/religion'. The parallel epithets are poetic praise attached to the named person.
segments.s007-g15: علی is the proper name 'Ali' and بود is the past copula 'was'. جفت بتول is a noun phrase 'jift-e Batul' meaning 'the pair/mate of Batul' (i.e. spouse/companion); جفت functions as an epithet attached to Ali. The ordering is standard for classical Persian: nominal predicate after the copula.
segments.s007-g16: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. او را is the object pronoun with the object marker را ('him'); به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'for/by goodness' ('because of his goodness'), where به functions as a prepositional/adverbial particle. ستاید is a classical present (literary/habitual) form of ستودن 'to praise' meaning 'praises' (here 'the Messenger praises him'); رسول at the end is the subject 'the Messenger' — in Persian poetry the verb often precedes the explicit subject for emphasis.
segments.s007-g17: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). The clause contains two nominal/copular statements in compressed poetic order: من (I) + شهر علمم ('the city of knowledge' — a nominal predicate) with the copula omitted for the first clause (typical in poetic Persian), then علی…در است where در ('door, gate') is the predicate and است is the copula at the end: 'Ali is the door (gate)'.
segments.s007-g18: درست is an adverb/adjective meaning 'true' or 'correct' modifying the whole following nominal phrase. این سخن قول پیغمبر is a genitive-type sequence meaning 'this saying (سخن) is the saying/word (قول) of the Prophet (پیغمبر)'; است is the final copula ('is').
segments.s007-g19: گواهی دهم is a performative present/personal construction 'I give testimony' (دهم = I give). کاین is an older fused form of 'که این' introducing the subordinate clause 'that these sayings are from him' (سخن‌ها از اوست), where از indicates source/attribution and ست/است is the copula.
segments.s007-g20: تو گویی here is an idiomatic/simile-introducing phrase meaning 'it is as if / one would say' rather than the literal 'you'. دو گوشم = 'my two ears' (دو = two, گوش + ـم = ear + my). پر + (آواز او) = 'full of his voice', and the clause ends with ست (est) as the copula: 'are full of his voice.'
segments.s007-g21: علی را is the marked object/topic ('to Ali' or 'Ali' with the object marker را) and چنین گفت = 'said thus' (he said so). و دیگر همین is a connective phrase: و = 'and', دیگر همین = 'furthermore / this very thing' (an emphatic or continuing remark).
segments.s007-g22: کز is a subordinating/causal particle meaning 'that/from which' or 'because of which'. ایشان ('they') is the subject; قوی شد is past tense 'became strong' (قوی = strong, شد = became). به هر گونه دین uses به + noun to mean 'in/with respect to every kind (هر گونه) of religion (دین)' — 'in every manner of religion.'
segments.s007-g23: This is a pair of similes: نبی آفتاب = 'the Prophet [is] the sun' (noun + noun used predicatively), و صحابان چو ماه = 'and the Companions like the moon' (چو = 'like/as'). The copula is omitted in the first clause (poetic compression) and the second is a direct simile.
segments.s007-g24: به هم‌بسته is a participial/adjectival phrase 'bound/tied to one another' (به + هم‌بسته), یک‌دگر = 'one another' (reciprocal), and راست راه = 'the straight / right way' where راست qualifies راه. The sequence describes their mutual bond and being on the correct path.
segments.s007-g41: همانا is an emphatic adverb meaning “verily/truly.” که introduces a content clause ('that...') here. باشد is a third-person present/subjunctive form of بودن used as a copula ('is/will be') in literary Persian. م + را together mark the first‑person object (م = enclitic for 'me', را = definite object marker); they attach to the predicate that follows. دستگیر is a noun here meaning 'helper/succorer' and functions as the predicate of باشد: literally 'that there is for me a helper.'
segments.s007-g42: This is a nominal apposition listing divine royal titles that complete the previous clause: خداوند (subject) followed by a list of titles/attributes. The repeated و simply links the items; the copula (is) is omitted as is common in poetry, so the whole phrase supplies who the 'helper' is.
segments.s007-g43: Here خداوند is the subject; جوی ('stream') is a predicate noun predicated of God (the copula 'is' is omitted as typical in verse). می‌و (a poetic form) and انگبین are the contents of that stream: 'wine (or fruit/wine) and nectar.' The verb/copula is implied rather than expressed.
segments.s007-g44: همان ('the same' / 'the very') points back to the preceding image, introducing چشمه ('spring, source') qualified by شیر ('milk') and ماء ('water') modified by معین. Again the copula is omitted: 'the same spring of milk and appointed water.' The adjective/participial sense of معین ('appointed/ordained') qualifies ماء.
segments.s007-g45: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' چشم here denotes the figurative 'eye/attention/gaze' (i.e., desire or looking toward), and داری is the present second‑person 'you have' (you direct/possess that gaze). به + دیگر سرای marks the target/destination ('toward another house/abode'). Together: 'If you have your eye on another abode...'
segments.s007-g46: به نزد means 'to/into the presence of'; نبی و علی are the persons named (the Prophet and Ali). گیر is an imperative/verbal form 'take/seize' (here 'take/find'), and جای is the noun 'place.' The phrase instructs the addressee to 'take a place in the presence of the Prophet and Ali.'
segments.s007-g47: گرت is a contracted conditional 'if (it be) for you' or 'if you...' زین ('from this/of this') + بد آید ('comes bad' = 'seems bad/offends') is an idiom meaning 'if this seems wrong/bad to you.' گناه من است is a simple copular clause: 'it is my sin/my fault.'
segments.s007-g48: چنین است is a short assertion 'thus it is.' The conjunction و links the next clause: این دین و راه من است — 'this religion and way/path is mine.' این is the demonstrative 'this,' دین 'religion/faith,' راه 'way/path,' and من است marks possession ('is mine').
segments.s007-g25: منم is the colloquial/poetic fusion of من + ام meaning “I am” (copula attached to the pronoun). بنده is a nominal self-reference ‘servant’ and is followed by the noun phrase اهل‌بیت نبی (the Prophet’s household)—the expected ezafe (ِ) is omitted in poetic diction, so the whole string reads “I am (a) servant of the Prophet’s household.”
segments.s007-g26: ستاینده is an active participle used as a noun ‘praiser’. خاک پای وصی is a compact genitive chain meaning ‘the dust (khāk) of the foot (pāy) of the Wasi/successor (وصی)’. In poetic word order the phrase is left without additional linking particles: ‘a praiser of the dust of the successor’s feet.’
segments.s007-g27: This clause follows an SOV/poetic order: حکیم (subject, a divine or exalted epithet 'the Wise One') + این جهان را (direct object; the object marker را emphasizes definiteness) + چو دریا (simile 'like a sea') + نهاد (past of نهادن 'placed/made'). نهاد is the past-tense verb meaning ‘set/made’ here: ‘made this world like a sea.’
segments.s007-g28: برانگیخته is a perfect/passive participle used predicatively ('roused, stirred up'), موج is its object ('waves'). In poetic inversion the agent تندباد ('gale') follows the phrase, so the line reads like a clause with implicit copula: ‘A gale has stirred up waves from it’ (از او refers back to the sea).
segments.s007-g29: چو introduces a comparison or temporal clause 'as/when'. The phrase (literally) ‘چو هفتاد کشتی بر او ساخته’ uses ساخته (past participle of ساختن) to mean ‘(were) made/set’ and بر او = 'on it' (the sea). Poetic omission of an explicit auxiliary/copy of the verb gives the sense 'as if seventy ships were set upon it.'
segments.s007-g30: همه بادبان‌ها is the noun phrase 'all the sails'. بر افراخته uses the preposition بر + the participle افراخته 'raised/hoisted' to mean 'with (their) sails hoisted'; again the participle stands in for a finite verb (nominal/predicative use) and agrees logically with the ships previously mentioned.
segments.s007-g31: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one' / 'a certain', followed by the adjective پهن before the noun کشتی ('a broad ship'). به سان is a fixed simile marker 'like/as', so the whole phrase reads 'one broad ship like a bride.' The adjective–noun order (پهن کشتی) is normal and the simile immediately follows.
segments.s007-g32: بیاراسته is used adjectivally to describe the ship as 'arrayed/adorned' (note orthographic/semantic ambiguity in Classical Persian; here it functions positively). همچو = 'such as/like' introduces a further simile, and چشم خروس 'the eye of the rooster' is the image invoked. The short noun phrases and participial adjective reflect poetic compression: 'arrayed like a bride, like the rooster’s eye.'
segments.s007-g33: This phrase lists two proper names with locative/associative markers: محمد is the principal name, بدو (به + او) functions as a clitic-like 'to/with him' or 'with him present', and اندرون 'inside/within' locates the following name; با introduces the accompanying person. Word order is name → pointer/locative → prepositional phrase (با + name). The whole cluster means 'Muhammad — with him, inside, Ali'.
segments.s007-g34: همان is the demonstrative 'the same/they are', introducing an identification; اهل‌بیت is the technical term 'the Prophet's household'; نبی here designates 'the Prophet' as title; و is the coordinating 'and'; ولی is used in the sense 'guardian/saint/successor' (not the adversative meaning). The phrase identifies Muhammad and Ali as the Prophet's household and guardian.
segments.s007-g35: خردمند is a noun/adjective 'wise person' serving as the subject; کز is the archaic/poetic relative particle (combination of که + ز) meaning 'who/that'; دور here is an adverbial 'from afar' modifying دریا 'sea'; بدید is the simple past 3rd-person 'saw'. Word order is subject → relative particle → distance phrase → object → verb (typical in older Persian poetry).
segments.s007-g36: This clause gives two nominal phrases negated/qualified by adjectives. کرانه 'shore' is followed by the negation نه + predicate پیدا 'visible' (نه پیدا = 'not visible'); و links the next noun بن 'bottom/base' which takes the adjective ناپدید 'unseen/vanished'. Adjective follows noun as is usual in Persian, and نه is the classical negator placed before the predicate.
segments.s007-g37: بدانست is the past tense 'he knew' (بد + دانست = 'he knew that'); کو here is the relative/interrogative 'where' (classical usage); موج is the subject 'wave'; خواهد + infinitive زدن is the future periphrasis 'will strike' (خواه + زدن = 'will/shall strike'). The clause means 'he knew where the wave would strike.'
segments.s007-g38: This clause expresses a negative prediction. کس is used in the sense 'no one' (here understood negatively by the following negated verbal phrase); از غرق 'from drowning' is a prepositional phrase; بیرون 'out' combines with the verb; نخواهد + شدن is a negated future periphrasis meaning 'will not become / will not be able to get out' — overall: 'no one will get out of drowning.'
segments.s007-g39: به دل گفت is an idiom 'he said to his heart', i.e., 'he thought to himself'; به introduces the internal locus (دل = 'heart, inner thought'); گفت is a past-tense verb 'he said' (here rendered as thought). اگر begins a conditional 'if'; با نبی و وصی is the prepositional phrase 'with the Prophet and the successor', the complement of the conditional. The clause sets up the condition for what follows.
segments.s007-g40: شوم is the subjunctive/potential 'if I become' triggered by the preceding اگر; غرقه is a predicative adjective/participle 'drenched/drowned' linked to that subjunctive (i.e. 'if I be drowned'); دارم is the present 'I have' and takes the following noun phrase دو یار وفی 'two faithful companions' (دو numeral before the noun as normal). وفی is an archaic/poetic adjective 'faithful'. The sequence expresses: 'If I am with them — even if I drown — I have two loyal friends.'
segments.s007-g49: بر این زادم literally places the preposition بر before the demonstrative این: this is a common classical Persian idiom meaning “I was born for this.” زادم is 1st‑person singular (the older form of 'زاد' with suffix -م) meaning “I was born” or “my origin is.” و هم بر این بگذرم repeats the frame with هم (“also/too”) and بگذرم (present/subjunctive form with ب‑) meaning “I will pass/spend/die for this” — the verb stands after the prepositional phrase, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s007-g50: چنان دان is an imperative/optative addressed to the addressee: دان here means “consider/accept (it) so.” که introduces the explanatory clause. خاک پی حیدرم is a compact poetic phrase: خاک (dust/earth) + پی (here = ‘foot/track; at the feet of’ in older usage) + حیدرم (حیدر + enclitic -م = “my Haydar” or simply Haydar with a possessive suffix used idiomatically). Word order places خاک first (the predicated identity) followed by the locus (پی حیدرِم).
segments.s007-g51: دلت‌گر is the fused form of دلت + گر, where گر is the conditional particle ‘if’; the phrase thus means “if your heart…” (literally “your-heart‑if”). به راه خطا مایل است is a predicative clause: به marks the goal/target ‘toward the path,’ خطا = ‘wrong/sinful,’ and مایل است = “is inclined” (مایل + copula است). The word order places the prepositional phrase before the adjective-verb, as usual.
segments.s007-g52: تو را uses را as a topicalizer/emphatic marker (‘as for you’). دشمن اندر جهان خود دل است has a slightly inverted order: دشمن (subject) + اندر جهان (locative adjunct ‘in the world’) + خود دل (literally ‘the very/own heart’) + است (copula). The sense: ‘your own heart will be (or is) your enemy in the world.’
segments.s007-g53: نباشد is a negative present/subjunctive form (na‑ + باشد) used impersonally here: ‘there will not be / there is not.’ جز از introduces the exception (‘except from’), and بی‌پدر functions as a derogatory epithet ‘fatherless’ (i.e., scoundrel or orphan). دشمنش is ‘his enemy’ (دشمن + enclitic -ش). The whole clause reads: ‘there will be none as his enemy except a fatherless one.’
segments.s007-g54: که introduces the clause giving the wished punishment. یزدان is the divine subject (‘God’). به آتش is the prepositional phrase marking the instrument/medium (‘into/with fire’). بسوزد is a jussive/subjunctive verb meaning ‘may (he) burn’ or ‘(God) burn (him),’ and تنش is ‘his body’ (تن + enclitic -ش). The clause expresses a curse: ‘that God burn his body in the fire.’
segments.s007-g55: هر آنکس که is the prototypical relative conditional ‘whoever.’ در جانش places the feeling inside the person’s inner being (در + جان + enclitic -ش = ‘in his soul/heart’). بغض علی‌ست is a verbless or copular structure where بغض (resentment/grudge) is linked to علی: the meaning is ‘there is a grudge against Ali in him’ (علی marked as the object of the grudge). The clause thus says ‘whoever has hatred of Ali in his soul.’
segments.s007-g56: از او زارتر is a comparative: از او = ‘than him,’ زارتر = ‘more wretched.’ در جهان زار کیست؟ is a rhetorical question: ‘who in the world is wretched?’ with زار used both as adjective and as a vocative of lament. The whole sentence reads: ‘whoever bears hatred of Ali in his soul — who in the world is more wretched than him?’
segments.s007-g57: 'نگر' is an imperative/imperial verb meaning 'look' or 'take care'; it introduces a warning clause. 'تا' here links that warning to the content that follows (roughly 'that' / 'lest'). 'نداری' is a noun/abstract meaning 'lack/poverty' (i.e. losing possessions); 'به' marks relation 'to/through', and 'بازی جهان' is a genitive phrase 'the play/game of the world' (worldly affairs). Together: 'Beware that you do not come to lack because of the world's play.'
segments.s007-g58: The clause is negative-prohibitive: 'نه' negates the following verb 'برگردی' (present/subjunctive/optative sense 'turn back / turn away'); together it means 'do not turn away'. 'از' marks separation 'from', and 'نیک‌پی همرهان' is a noun phrase 'well‑born (نیک‌پی) companions (همرهان)'. So: 'do not turn away from well‑born companions.'
segments.s007-g59: 'همه نیکی‌ات' means 'all your goodness / all your good deeds' (possessive suffix -ات). 'باید' is the modal 'must / ought to'. The verb phrase 'آغاز کرد' is the infinitive/periphrastic construction 'to begin / one must begin' (آغاز + کردن), so the clause says 'all your goodness must be begun.'
segments.s007-g60: 'چو' here functions as 'when' (a temporal/conditional conjunction). 'با نیک‌نامان' = 'with the reputable / well‑named (people)'. The word glossed 'بوی' in the word list does not fit the literal sense 'smell' here; in classical diction it behaves as an archaic/be‑form ('be' / 'are') linking to 'هم‌نورد' so the phrase reads 'when (you) are fellow‑travellers with the reputable.' 'هم‌نورد' means 'fellow‑traveler / comrade.'
segments.s007-g61: از این در is a fronted prepositional/topic phrase meaning “concerning this matter” (از = concerning + این = this + در = in/subject). سخن چند رانم همی literally orders noun + quantifier + verb: سخن چند (“a few words”) is the object/phrase being introduced, رانم is the 1st-person singular present of راندن (‘I drive/utter’ here = ‘I utter’), and همی is an archaic poetic verbal particle that marks continuous/habitual or prospective aspect (here: ‘I will / I am about to’). The subject is omitted (understood “I”), and word order reflects classical Persian/poetic inversion.
segments.s007-g62: همانا is an emphatic/introductory particle meaning “verily/truly.” کرانش is کران + -ش (the noun “limit/bound” with the 3rd-person possessive suffix “its”). ندانم is the negative present form (ن + دانم) meaning “I do not know”; the order places the verb before any explicit subject (typical in classical verse). The final همی is the same archaic particle used for emphasis/aspect here, giving an emphatic sense (“indeed / at all”).
segments.s007-g63: This is colloquial/modern Persian recounting Ferdowsi’s words. "دینش رو" uses the enclitic "ش" (his) on "دین" and the colloquial object marker "رو" (instead of literary "را"). "تبعیت از نبی و علی ذکر کرده" is a nominal phrase + perfective: "has mentioned (that) [the] following of the Prophet and Ali." "در ضمن گفته" = "in addition he said." The following conditional clauses use colloquial forms: "اگه... هستی/نداری/داری" are present-tense conditions; "نمیخواد ناراحت بشی" uses "نمیخواد" idiomatically as "it is not necessary" (or "there is no need") + subjunctive "بشی." "این گناه من هست و خودم پیش خدا پاسخگو هستم" is a present-tense copular claim taking the speaker as personally accountable before God. "گناه من رو پای تو نمی‌نویسند که بخوای از من طلبکار بشی" uses figurative phrasing: "پای تو نوشتن" = "to record/attribute (the blame) to you," with purpose/result clause introduced by "که" + subjunctive "بخوای...بشی." The rest gives the speaker's stance and imperatives: past-tense justification "این فهم من بوده لذا این دین رو انتخاب کردم," then prohibitive and imperative commands "تو خودتو ناراحت نکن" / "بجاش برو تحقیق کن و به دیگران گیر نده," closing with the formulaic "والسلام."
segments.s007-g64: This is a short, somewhat archaic/poetic statement. "گرت" is an older contraction meaning "اگر تو" (if you); "زین" is a classical form for "از این" (from/ because of this); "بد آید" literally "comes bad" = "seems bad/it displeases you." "گناه منست" is a compact copular phrase (poetic contraction of "گناهِ من است") meaning "the fault/sin is mine." "چنین است" = "so it is," and "و این دین و راه منست" repeats the copula to state "and this religion and way are mine." Overall the clause asserts that if this displeases you, the blame belongs to the speaker and the choice stands.

sentences.s007-s1: Knowledge and religion will rightly deliver you; you must seek salvation.
sentences.s007-s2: And if your heart does not wish to be base, you should not desire to remain forever in the guise of the needy.
sentences.s007-s3: Follow your Prophet's words and seek the path; wash your heart of darkness with this water.
sentences.s007-s4: What did that Lord of revelation and inspiration say — the Lord of command and the Lord of prohibition?
sentences.s007-s5: For after the Messenger no one shone like Abu Bakr.
sentences.s007-s6: Umar made Islam manifest and adorned the world like a spring garden.
sentences.s007-s7: After those two, there was Uthmān, the chosen one, master of modesty and master of the faith.
sentences.s007-s8: Fourth was Ali, the mate of Batul, whom the Messenger praises for his goodness.
sentences.s007-s9: That I am the city of knowledge and Ali is its gate; this saying of the Prophet is true.
sentences.s007-s10: I give testimony that these sayings are from him; it is as if both my ears are full of his voice.
sentences.s007-s11: He said this to Ali; and because of them religion became strong in every way.
sentences.s007-s12: The Prophet is the sun and the Companions like the moon; bound to one another, they are on the straight (right) path.
sentences.s007-s21: Truly, God will be my helper — the Lord of crown, banner, and throne.
sentences.s007-s22: The Lord is a stream of wine and nectar — the very spring of milk and appointed water.
sentences.s007-s23: If you cast your eye toward another abode, take a place in the presence of the Prophet and Ali.
sentences.s007-s24: If this displeases you, it is my sin; such is it, and this is my religion and way.
sentences.s007-s13: I am a servant of the Prophet's household, a praiser of the dust of the successor's feet.
sentences.s007-s14: The Wise One made this world like the sea; a gale has stirred up waves from it.
sentences.s007-s15: As if seventy ships were on it made (set upon it), all their sails raised.
sentences.s007-s16: One broad ship, like a bride, arrayed—like the eye of a rooster.
sentences.s007-s17: Muhammad — with him, inside, Ali; they are the Prophet's household and (his) guardian.
sentences.s007-s18: A wise man who saw the sea from afar; the shore was not visible and the bottom unseen.
sentences.s007-s19: He knew where the wave would strike; no one would come out of drowning.
sentences.s007-s20: He said to his heart: if I am with the Prophet and the appointed successor — even if I drown, I have two faithful companions.
sentences.s007-s25: I was born for this, and for this I will also pass away; consider that I am the dust at Haydar's feet.
sentences.s007-s26: If your heart is inclined toward the wrong path, your very heart will be your enemy in the world.
sentences.s007-s27: There will be no enemy of his except a fatherless one; may God burn his body in the fire.
sentences.s007-s28: Whoever has hatred of Ali in his soul — who in the world is more wretched than he?
sentences.s007-s29: Take care that you do not lose (or lack) anything in the world's play; do not turn away from well‑born companions.
sentences.s007-s30: All your goodness must be begun when you are fellow‑travellers with the well‑reputed.
sentences.s007-s31: On this matter I will utter a few words; truly, I do not know its limit.
sentences.s007-s32: Ferdowsi described his religion as following the Prophet and Ali; he also said that if you are Sunni and do not like this doctrine, or if you belong to any other sect or rite, there is no need for you to be upset — this is my sin and I myself am answerable before God. They will not record my sin as being on you so that you could make a claim against me. This has been my understanding, therefore I chose this faith. Do not trouble yourself; instead go and investigate, and do not bother others — and that is all.
